notes.note: Astura was a small island and villa at the mouth of the river of the same name on the Latian coast, where Cicero withdrew in near-total seclusion after Tullia's death in February 45 BC. He found its emptiness more endurable than company, even as it left him alone with his grief.

notes.anchor: I would rather have him a temple-mate of Quirinus
notes.note: In 45 BC a statue of Caesar was set up in the temple of Quirinus (the deified Romulus), inscribed to him as a god "sharing the temple" (Greek synnaos). Cicero's barb is double-edged: Quirinus was the deified Romulus, who was also said to have been torn apart by the senators, so to wish Caesar a temple-mate of Quirinus rather than of Salus (Welfare) is to hint darkly at his end.

The temple of Salus (the goddess of Health and public Welfare) stood on the Quirinal next to Atticus's house. Cicero plays Salus off against Quirinus: he would sooner see Caesar lodged with the ominous Quirinus than honoured as a guarantor of the state's welfare.

Cicero urges Atticus to circulate Hirtius's pamphlet attacking Cato, part of the literary war touched off by Cicero's own eulogy the Cato. He reckons the attack will misfire: while readers admire Hirtius's ability, the very project of running Cato down will only make it look ridiculous.
